messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] Survival and Time-to-Event Data Analysis
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-EPI members

Discover how to effectively analyze time-to-event data and enhance your epidemiological research skills. Introduction to Survival and Time-to-Event Data Analysis is a 2-day seminar livestreaming May 21, led by Centre for Applied Statistics Courses (CASC) (University College London). This seminar offers a comprehensive introduction to survival and time-to-event data analysis, essential for understanding event occurrence and its predictors in epidemiological studies. Researchers in epidemiology can apply these methods, including Kaplan-Meier and Cox regression, to analyze outcomes like disease progression, recovery times, or intervention effectiveness using R. Participants will gain practical skills in data preparation, model interpretation, and academic write-ups, directly enhancing their ability to conduct robust time-to-event analyses in their own research.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/introduction-to-survival-and-time-to-eve-1) to secure your spot, and please share this opportunity with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] Last chance: R for Exploratory Multivariate Analysis
messages.subject_clean: Last chance: R for Exploratory Multivariate Analysis
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-EPI members

Last chance - This seminar offers a unique opportunity to enhance your quantitative research skills. Exploratory Multivariate Analysis in R is a 2-day seminar livestreaming May 7 and 20 with SMCS (Universit? Catholique de Louvain). This seminar introduces exploratory multivariate analysis with a focus on practical implementation in R, covering techniques like PCA, EFA, and clustering. For epidemiologists, these methods are invaluable for reducing dimensionality in complex health datasets, identifying hidden patterns, and handling diverse data types often encountered in public health research. Applying these skills will enable you to strengthen empirical claims, enhance reproducibility, and improve the clarity of published findings in your own epidemiological studies.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/exploratory-multivariate-analysis-in-r) to register now, and we'd appreciate you sharing this with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] Last chance: Modelling and Mapping Trait Evolution in R
messages.subject_clean: Last chance: Modelling and Mapping Trait Evolution in R
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-EPI members

Last chance - Discover how to apply advanced phylogenetic comparative methods to your research questions. Modelling and Mapping Trait Evolution is a 2-day seminar livestreaming May 11 and 13 with Renan Maestri (Department of Ecology, Universidade Federal do Rio Grande do Sul). This seminar introduces statistical and computational methods for modelling and mapping trait evolution on phylogenies, providing practical R workflows for robust analysis. Researchers in epidemiology can apply these phylogenetic comparative methods to understand disease evolution, track pathogen spread, or analyze host-pathogen co-evolutionary dynamics. The skills acquired, including ancestral state reconstruction and multi-rate model fitting using R packages, will enhance your ability to infer evolutionary processes and produce publication-ready visualizations of epidemiological trends.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/modelling-and-mapping-trait-evolution) to join before enrollment closes, and please pass this to colleagues and students who may be interested!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] Last chance: Practical Regression Modeling Strategies
messages.subject_clean: Last chance: Practical Regression Modeling Strategies
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-EPI members

Last chance - We invite you to explore contemporary strategies for robust multivariable regression modeling in your research. Regression Modeling Strategies is a 2-day seminar livestreaming May 14 and 19 with Frank Harrell (Department of Biostatistics, Vanderbilt University) and Drew Levy. This seminar offers contemporary strategies for specifying, fitting, validating, and interpreting multivariable regression models. Researchers in epidemiology can apply these methods to address common challenges like nonlinearity, overfitting, missing data, and to effectively model continuous, binary, ordinal, and survival outcomes in their R-based analyses. The practical guidance on principled model formulation, causal inference, and reproducible analysis, including R code examples, will enhance the credibility and impact of your empirical work.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/regression-modeling-strategies) to reserve your place before it starts, and feel free to circulate this with colleagues and students!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org
	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] Interactive Maps with R – online course next week
messages.subject_clean: Interactive Maps with R – online course next week
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
If you are interested in learning how to create and customizse interactive maps in R, you can still join us for this 2-day online course: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/interactive-maps-with-r/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/interactive-maps-with-r/ ) 
 
Dates and time: 13-14 May (3-7 PM Berlin time).
 
Interactive maps are increasingly used as powerful tools for communicating spatial information, allowing users to zoom, pan, explore multiple layers, and interact dynamically with data. In this course, participants will learn how to build and publish interactive maps using free and open-source R packages such as leaflet and mapview.
 
The course combines short theoretical introductions with extensive hands-on practical exercises. Participants will work with both provided and personal spatial datasets, learning how to create multi-layer interactive maps, customize map elements, add charts and pop-up information, synchronize maps, and publish their outputs online for collaborators or the public.
 
Best regards,
Carlo

--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D


Physalia-courses DIRECTOR

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] Last chance: R, Regression, and rms Package
messages.subject_clean: Last chance: R, Regression, and rms Package
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-EPI members

Last chance - This seminar offers a comprehensive introduction to statistical computing and regression analysis. Introduction to R, Regression, and the rms Package is a 2-day seminar livestreaming May 11 with Frank Harrell (Department of Biostatistics, Vanderbilt University) and Drew Levy. This workshop introduces foundational R and RStudio skills, linear and multiple regression principles, and the rms package for robust model specification and diagnosis. Epidemiological researchers can apply these methods to analyze complex health data, develop predictive models, and rigorously assess risk factors or treatment effects. The seminar also emphasizes building reproducible analysis workflows with Quarto, ensuring transparency and facilitating collaboration in your research projects.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/introduction-to-r-regression-and-the-rms) to secure your spot, and please share this opportunity with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] Dealing with Messy Data in R – Online Course (13–15 July)
messages.subject_clean: Dealing with Messy Data in R – Online Course (13–15 July)
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
Want to learn how to analyse real-world data with R?
Join our online course ?Dealing with Messy Data in R: Data Cleaning and Visualisation? taking place on 13?15 July.
 
In this hands-on course, you will learn how to clean, transform, and visualise messy datasets using the tidyverse and ggplot2, and how to turn raw data into clear, publication-ready figures.
 
The course is designed for participants who want to strengthen their data wrangling and visualisation skills. No prior experience with tidyverse or ggplot2 is required.
 
 
Course website: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/messy-data/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/messy-data/ ) 
 
Best regards,
Carlo

--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] Last chance: Practical Web Scraping and Data Collection
messages.subject_clean: Last chance: Practical Web Scraping and Data Collection
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-EPI members

Last chance - Explore methods for extracting and transforming web-based data into robust inputs for your empirical studies. Web Scraping and Data Collection is a 2-day seminar livestreaming May 13 and 15 with Moses Boudourides (Data Science Graduate Program, School of Professional Studies, Northwestern University). This seminar offers a rigorous introduction to web scraping and structured data collection, focusing on transforming diverse online sources into analysis-ready datasets. Researchers in epidemiology can apply these methods to access contemporary behavioral, institutional, and textual data, enabling bespoke longitudinal and cross-sectional research designs for their studies. Participants will learn to design robust collection strategies, implement cleaning and validation routines, and document data provenance, enhancing the credibility and reproducibility of their empirical epidemiological work.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/web-scraping-and-data-collection) to register now, and we'd appreciate you sharing this with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org
	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] Bayesian Methods for Biomedical Data Science (Online Course | Nov 30 – Dec 4)
messages.subject_clean: Bayesian Methods for Biomedical Data Science (Online Course | Nov 30 – Dec 4)
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
 
We are pleased to announce the upcoming online course ?Bayesian Methods for Biomedical Data Science?, taking place from November 30 to December 4.
 
Course website: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/bayesian-in-compgen/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/bayesian-in-compgen/ ) 
 
 
Uncertainty is inherent to biomedical data, and Bayesian methods offer a principled and powerful framework to model it. This 5-day hands-on course is designed to equip participants with both the theoretical foundations and practical skills needed to apply Bayesian approaches in modern biomedical research.
 
The course is intended for advanced MSc students, PhD candidates, and postdoctoral researchers in statistics, biostatistics, and computational biology.
 
Topics include:

? Bayesian inference & probabilistic modeling
? MCMC methods (Gibbs sampling, Hamiltonian Monte Carlo) and diagnostics
? Hierarchical models, GLMs, and Bayesian regularization
? Variational inference
? Multimodal data integration and uncertainty quantification
 
 
All concepts will be reinforced through practical sessions using real biomedical datasets, ensuring participants gain hands-on experience with real-world applications.


 
Best regards,
Carlo
 
 

--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D


Physalia-courses DIRECTOR

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] AI-Assisted Systematic Reviews & Meta-Analysis
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-EPI members

Discover how to enhance your research efficiency and rigor through advanced AI-assisted methods for literature synthesis. AI-Assisted Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analysis is a 2-day seminar livestreaming June 10 and 12, led by Moses Boudourides (Data Science Graduate Program, School of Professional Studies, Northwestern University). This seminar introduces AI-assisted systematic review and meta-analytic workflows, providing a rigorous, no-code approach to managing extensive bodies of literature without sacrificing transparency or reproducibility. Epidemiologists can leverage these methods to efficiently synthesize evidence from vast scholarly outputs, enhancing the speed and defensibility of their own systematic reviews and meta-analyses. Participants will gain practical skills in corpus construction, AI-assisted screening, and structured data extraction, enabling more robust and timely evidence synthesis for dissertations, grants, and publications in their field.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/ai-assisted-systematic-reviews-and-meta) to reserve your place, and feel free to circulate this with colleagues and students who may be interested!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] Online Course: Bayesian Causal Networks in R
messages.subject_clean: Online Course: Bayesian Causal Networks in R
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
We are pleased to announce our upcoming online course: Bayesian Causal Networks in R
 
 
Dates: 7?11 September  -3?6 PM (Berlin time)
 
Course website: [ https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/bayesian-netwok/ ]( https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/bayesian-netwok/ ) 
 
Bayesian causal networks (Bayesian belief networks) are powerful probabilistic graphical models used to analyse complex multivariate systems under uncertainty. They combine causal reasoning with quantitative data analysis, enabling robust inference, prediction, and decision-making across a wide range of scientific disciplines.
 
This course provides a practical and comprehensive introduction to Bayesian causal modelling in R. Through lectures, live coding sessions, and guided exercises, participants will learn how to:
 
1) Formulate causal assumptions using directed acyclic graphs (DAGs)

2) Perform structure and parameter learning in Bayesian networks

3) Handle missing data and discretisation strategies
 
4) Conduct posterior inference and sensitivity analyses

5) Validate and interpret causal models for scientific applications
 
Participants will build and evaluate their own multivariate Bayesian network using real and simulated datasets.
 
 
 
Best regards,
Carlo

--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D


Physalia-courses DIRECTOR
